title: Assembling a Simple CNN Block by Block
concept: A toy CNN becomes easy to follow when you read it block by block: input image, a few conv/ReLU/pool stages, a compact head, and then the final prediction scores.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which layer is the most common simple activation inside beginner CNN blocks?
exercises.explain: Correct — ReLU is the usual beginner-friendly activation in simple CNN blocks.

cards.title: Loose parts become a working path
cards.body: A CNN diagram stops feeling abstract once you stop seeing isolated layers and start seeing a route: the image enters, passes through a few repeating blocks, gets summarized, and then becomes a prediction.

cards.title: One toy CNN, one clear route
cards.body: A beginner CNN might look like 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> Conv(16) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> GlobalAvgPool -> Dense -> 3 logits. The first block learns simple patterns, the second block learns richer ones, and the head turns the final feature summary into class scores.

cards.title: Drills first, scoreboard last
cards.body: A sports practice is not just the final score. Players warm up, run drills, combine skills, and only then play the game. CNNs do something similar: early stages prepare and combine visual clues before the head gives the final answer.

cards.title: Walk through the toy network
cards.body: Start with a 32x32x3 RGB image. Conv(8) creates 8 feature maps that may respond to simple edges or textures. ReLU keeps the response nonlinear. MaxPool halves the map to 16x16x8. A second Conv(16) builds richer features, another pool shrinks to 8x8x16, GlobalAvgPool squeezes each channel to one number, and the dense layer outputs 3 logits — one score per class.

cards.title: A real CNN does not need ten fancy blocks
cards.body: Beginners sometimes assume a network is only 'real' if it looks huge. But even a tiny CNN with two feature blocks can tell the full story of feature extraction, downsampling, and prediction.

cards.title: Feature extractor vs. prediction head
cards.body: The first part builds features. The last part reads them.

left.text:
Conv -> ReLU -> Pool
Conv -> ReLU -> Pool
Turn pixels into learned features

right.text:
GlobalAvgPool -> Dense
Compress final channels
Output class scores

exercises.prompt: In the toy sequence Conv(8) -> ReLU -> MaxPool, which step most directly comes right after the convolution?
exercises.explain: Right — in this simple block, ReLU follows the convolution before pooling shrinks the map.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Global average pooling usually appears near the prediction head, not before the very first convolution.
exercises.explain: True — global average pooling is a late summarizing step, not an opening move.
Head is the right word — it is the end piece that reads the learned features and outputs scores.
exercises.sentence: After the feature blocks, the classifier ___ reads the final features and outputs logits.

exercises.prompt: Which walkthrough of a toy CNN is correct?
exercises.explain: Exactly — that answer follows the data in the correct order from image to prediction.

options.text: Image -> feature blocks -> pooled feature summary -> dense output scores
Image -> final class label -> maybe some convolutions later
Image -> pooling forever -> no prediction head needed

exercises.prompt: A friend asks you to explain this toy model: 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> ReLU -> Pool -> Conv(16) -> ReLU -> Pool -> GlobalAvgPool -> Dense -> 3 logits. Which explanation is best?
exercises.explain: Exactly. That is the clean block-by-block story: early blocks build features, pooling shrinks them, the later block makes richer channels, and the head turns the final summary into class scores.

options.text: The image passes through two feature blocks that learn and shrink patterns, then a compact head converts the final channels into 3 class scores
The model predicts the label before learning features, then uses convolutions only to decorate the answer
Pooling is the only layer that matters, and the dense head is optional because CNNs do not need outputs
The 3 logits are three new images, not class scores
